trajectory_summary: The call is a clean mechanical execution — verify completed quickly, bill collected, appointment booked in-person for the next evening. But the call never built energy: no curiosity was created, no bill pain was anchored emotionally, the customer's channel preference was overridden without a value bridge, the son on title was not included in the appointment, and multiple verify items were skipped. The booking exists; its durability is uncertain.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked in-person appointment for 7:30 the following evening. However, the son is on the title and was not included in the appointment — per protocols.spouse_decision / protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, this is a structurally vulnerable booking. The outcome tag should reflect 'booked but fragile' rather than a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. But it's my son. I guess so.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The son is on the title and was not included in the appointment. Per protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, this is the same structural risk as excluding a spouse — the closer arrives at an in-person visit where a co-decision-maker may not be present. This is the single gap most likely to kill the sit. Carter needs to hear the protocol clearly: 'on the title' means 'on the appointment,' full stop.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. But it's my son. I guess so.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer identifies a son on the title. Carter acknowledged it ('Your son. I make it a good one. Yeah. Cool.') and moved immediately to the credit question. The son was never included in the appointment. Per the schema, a co-decision-maker on title must be present at the close — booking without them is structurally fragile.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Did you put in a request to get some information on solar?
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter never introduced himself as being with Ion Solar, never used the Tesla framing available for legitimacy, and never asked what interested the customer in solar (verify.interest_in_solar). The call opened as a callback confirmation and immediately became a data-collection exercise. The customer has no emotional reason to show up to the appointment.

Carter confirmed the utility company and immediately pivoted to bill format (mail vs online) and bill collection. The average bill was never asked verbally. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the bill must be captured AND emotionally flipped — but here the verbal anchor ('Do you know how much you're paying on average?') was skipped entirely before the bill-collection slog began. Carter has no bill-pain number to work with even as a conversational anchor.

key_moments.quote: K. Awesome. Yeah. So just for townhomes, you do need to own your own section of the roof and have your own electric meter. Is that the case for you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter correctly identified the townhome ownership question but never followed up with roof type or age — a hard DQ condition per the schema. On a townhome, roof age is particularly relevant because the customer may not control the full roof structure. The question was never asked at any point in the call.
